content.full_text:
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
322·5660 
Assemblyman Gray Davis 
State Capitol, Room 3126 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Assemblyman Davis: 
Executive/legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
322·5901 
322-6<Ul 
March 31, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Thank you for your request for information on the disclosure 
and disqualification provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
You were recently married and your questions concern wedding 
gifts that you and your spouse received. 
In response to your disclosure questions, I have enclosed 
copies of the Torres opinion, 2 FPPC Opinions 31 (No. 75-163) 
and a letter to Mr. Grover McKean (A-78-259). 
Both of these 
specifically discuss the reporting of wedding gifts and I think 
that they will answer your questions. 
The general rulell on 
reporting a wedding gift is that each spouse is considered to 
have an equal interest in the gift and the official must 
disclose his interest when it exceeds the $50 threshold. 
This 
means that you will report a gift if its value is $100 or 
more.~1 
with regard to your disqualification question, you indicated 
that some of the persons who provided you with gifts are 
individuals who may have financial interests in legislation 
coming before the Assembly. 
You asked when you must disqualify 
11 
See the Torres Opinion for the exceptions to this 
general rule. 
~I Please note that Torres and McKean were written when 
the threshold amount for reporting gifts was $25. 
The threshold 
was increased to $50 last year. 
If you received any gift from a lobbyist, the gift's value 
cannot exceed $20. 
(See Government Code Section 86203.1.1.) 
11 
All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise noted. 


Assemblyman Gray Davis 
Ma r c h 31, 198 3 
Page 2 
yourself and how much effort you must expend in determining 
whether a piece of legislation will affect a person who provided 
you with a gift. 
Section 87100 prohibits a state public official from making, 
participating in the making, or in any way attempting to use his 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which 
he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. 
An 
official has a financial interest in a decision within the 
meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material effect, distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally, on: 
. . . 
(c) 
Any sourc e 0 f income • . • aggr ega ting two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value 
provided to, received by or promised to the public 
official within 12 months prior to the time when the 
decision is made. 
Gifts constitute "income" under the Act. 
Income of an 
official includes a community property interest in the income of 
a spouse. 
(Section 82030 (a) .) 
Because gifts received by a 
spouse during marriage are his or her separate property (Cal. 
Const., Art. If Sec. 21; Civil Code Section 5107), your wife's 
share of your wedding gifts is not counted in determining 
whether you have received $250 in income from a single source. 
Thus, to satisfy the $250 threshold and trigger the 
disqualification requirements, a guest's gift must have a value 
of $500 or more. 
The meaning of the phrases "know or has reason to know," 
"material financial effect," and "public generally," are 
explained in the enclosed copies of letters to John Cohan, 
A-82-197 and Adriana Gianturco, A-81-090 (discussing "know or 
has reason to know"), and copies of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 
18702 and 18703 (discussing, respectively, "material financial 
effect" and the "public generally" exception). 
I hope I have answered your questions for you. 
If I can be 
of any further help, please do not hesitate to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM: nrw 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
JIVUS 5h~al~ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: Assemblyman Davis asked about disclosure requirements for wedding gifts received by him and his spouse, and when he must disqualify himself from voting on legislation that may affect persons who gave him gifts.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Wedding gifts must be disclosed by the official when their value exceeds $50 (or $100 if shared equally with spouse as separate property). Disqualification under Section 87100 is triggered only when a single gift source exceeds $500 in value, since spousal gifts are treated as separate property and not aggregated for the $250 income threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document has no explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION section headers but contains clear question and answer structure in letter format. Synthesis based on letter content and recipient context. References enclosed prior opinions (Torres, McKean, Cohan, Gianturco) and administrative code sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Assemblyman Davis asked about disclosure requirements for wedding gifts received by him and his spouse, and when he must disqualify himself from voting on legislation that may affect persons who gave him gifts.

CONCLUSION: Wedding gifts must be disclosed by the official when their value exceeds $50 (or $100 if shared equally with spouse as separate property). Disqualification under Section 87100 is triggered only when a single gift source exceeds $500 in value, since spousal gifts are treated as separate property and not aggregated for the $250 income threshold.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to Assemblyman Gray Davis regarding disclosure and disqualification obligations for wedding gifts received by him and his spouse, providing guidance on reporting thresholds and financial interest calculations under the Political Reform Act.
